topic: Civil War and Revolution in China
readings: Women, Communism, and Revolution (He Zhen, Chen Duxiu, Chiang Kai-shek, Mao Zedong)

readingAnalyses.title: What Women Should Know About Communism
readingAnalyses.author: He Zhen
readingAnalyses.summary: He Zhen, an anarchist-feminist writing from exile in Japan (early 1900s), argues that women's oppression stems not from men but from economic dependence. She categorizes suffering women: servants, factory workers, prostitutes, concubines, widows. Her radical solution is communism - the abolition of money and private property - not individual advancement through education or employment, which she sees as merely shifting dependence from husbands to employers.

keyQuotes.quote: My fellow women: do not hate men! Hate that you do not have food to eat.
As long as you depend on others, you cannot be free.

If every single woman understands that nothing is more evil than money, and they all unite together to cooperate with men to utterly overthrow the rich and powerful and then abolish money, then absolutely nothing will be allowed for individuals to own privately.

thematicExamples: The 'rice bowl' as symbol of dependence - women endure abuse to eat
Critique of liberal feminism: education and factory work just shift dependence from husband to employer/owner
Vision of communism as taking 'water from the ocean' - abundance without private ownership
Categories of women's suffering: servants, workers, prostitutes, concubines, widows
realLifeConnections: Gig economy and precarious labor - dependence on employers vs. husbands
Debates about 'lean in' feminism vs. systemic change
Universal Basic Income discussions - addressing economic dependence
Intersectionality: class and gender oppression intertwined

readingAnalyses.title: Our Final Awakening
readingAnalyses.author: Chen Duxiu
readingAnalyses.source: Changing China: Readings in the History of China, p. 168
readingAnalyses.summary: Chen Duxiu (1879-1942), later co-founder of the Chinese Communist Party, writes this New Youth article in 1916 calling for China's 'final awakening.' He argues that constitutional government cannot be granted from above by 'dignitaries and influential elders' - it must derive from the 'conscious realization and voluntary action of the majority.' A republic without changed consciousness is 'bogus constitutionalism' and 'political window-dressing.'

keyQuotes.quote: Our task today can be said to be the intense combat between the old and the modern currents of thought.
A constitutional republic which does not derive from the conscious realization and voluntary action of the majority of the people is a bogus republic and bogus constitutionalism.
It is political window-dressing, in no way like the republican constitutionalism of the countries of Europe and America.

thematicExamples: Critique of elite-led reform - constitutional government cannot be 'carried on the backs of a few'
May Fourth emphasis on consciousness change before political change
Comparison between old hopes for benevolent emperors and new hopes for enlightened elites
realLifeConnections: Debates about top-down vs. bottom-up political reform globally
Democracy promotion and its limits - can democracy be 'exported'?
Mass consciousness and social movements - BLM, climate activism
China's current government: does legitimacy require democratic participation?

readingAnalyses.title: Essentials of the New Life Movement
readingAnalyses.author: Chiang Kai-shek (Jiang Jieshi)
readingAnalyses.summary: Chiang Kai-shek's 1934 speech launches the New Life Movement, calling for moral reform based on four Confucian virtues: li (ritual/decorum), yi (rightness), lian (integrity), and chi (shame). He diagnoses Chinese society as 'spiritless' - lacking discrimination between good and evil, public and private, primary and secondary. His remedy is personal discipline, military-mindedness, and readiness to 'die for the country at any moment.' This represents the Nationalist alternative to Communist revolution.

keyQuotes.quote: The general psychology of our people today can be described as spiritless.
The four virtues are the essential principles for the promotion of morality. They form the major rules for dealing with men and human affairs.
We must preserve order, emphasize organization, responsibility and discipline, and be ready to die for the country at any moment.

thematicExamples: Confucian vocabulary repurposed for nationalist goals
Individual moral reform as solution to national weakness - blame the people, not the system
Context: Japan has seized Manchuria, Communists active in countryside, warlords control much of country
realLifeConnections: Authoritarian responses to social crisis - order over freedom
Personal responsibility rhetoric vs. structural change
Compare to Singapore's 'Asian values' or China's current 'civilization' campaigns
Nationalism and discipline: similar patterns in fascist movements

readingAnalyses.title: Reform Our Study
readingAnalyses.author: Mao Zedong
readingAnalyses.source: Selected Works of Mao Tse-tung, Vol. 3
readingAnalyses.summary: Mao's 1941 speech at Yan'an launches the Rectification Movement, criticizing 'subjectivism' - studying Marxism-Leninism in the abstract without connecting it to Chinese conditions. He attacks returned students who 'can only parrot things foreign' and 'become gramophones.' True Marxism requires integrating universal theory with 'concrete practice of the Chinese revolution.' This speech establishes Mao's authority to define correct ideology and begins the pattern of ideological campaigns.

keyQuotes.quote: To behave like 'a blindfolded man catching sparrows', or 'a blind man groping for fish', to be crude and careless, to indulge in verbiage, to rest content with a smattering of knowledge - such is the extremely bad style of work.
There are many Marxist-Leninist scholars who cannot open their mouths without citing ancient Greece; but as for their own ancestors - sorry, they have been forgotten.
Coming home from Europe, America or Japan, they can only parrot things foreign. They become gramophones and forget their duty to understand and create new things.

thematicExamples: Sinification of Marxism - adapting foreign theory to Chinese conditions
Anti-intellectualism: returned students as 'gramophones' parroting foreign ideas
Unity of theory and practice vs. 'subjectivism'
Yan'an as laboratory for CCP ideology and governance
realLifeConnections: Debates about applying Western theories to non-Western contexts
Populist critiques of 'out of touch' intellectuals
China today: Xi Jinping thought and ideological campaigns

overallSynthesis:
These four documents represent competing visions for China's transformation in the first half of the 20th century. He Zhen's anarchist-feminist vision calls for abolishing money and property. Chen Duxiu demands consciousness change before political reform. Chiang Kai-shek offers moral regeneration through Confucian virtues and military discipline. Mao Zedong insists on adapting Marxism to Chinese conditions through practical investigation.

What unites them is the diagnosis: China is in crisis, the old order is failing, something must fundamentally change. What divides them is the prescription. He Zhen sees capitalism and patriarchy as the enemy. Chen sees traditional consciousness as the obstacle. Chiang sees moral decay requiring discipline. Mao sees incorrect ideology requiring rectification.

By 1949, Mao's vision had won. But understanding why requires seeing the alternatives: Why didn't anarchism take hold? Why couldn't Chen's May Fourth liberalism translate into political power? Why did Chiang's moral campaigns fail to save the Nationalist government? The answer lies in who could mobilize the peasantry, who could build effective organizations, and who could offer a compelling vision of China's future.

Note the irony: Chen Duxiu co-founded the CCP but was expelled in 1929. Chiang's Nationalists would retreat to Taiwan in 1949. He Zhen disappeared from history. Only Mao's interpretation of Marxism became state orthodoxy - and even that would be radically revised after his death.

keywords.definition: Student-led protests and intellectual movement attacking traditional culture, demanding science and democracy
keywords.relevance: Chen Duxiu was a leader; movement shaped both Nationalist and Communist ideologies

keywords.definition: Chiang Kai-shek's campaign for moral regeneration based on Confucian virtues
keywords.relevance: Nationalist alternative to Communist revolution; failed to address structural problems

keywords.definition: CCP ideological campaign establishing Mao's authority and 'correct' interpretation of Marxism
keywords.relevance: Created template for later campaigns; established Mao Zedong Thought

keywords.definition: Mao's term for applying theory without investigating concrete conditions
keywords.relevance: Key accusation in rectification; opposite of 'seeking truth from facts'

keywords.definition: Confucian virtues (ritual, rightness, integrity, shame) promoted by Chiang
keywords.relevance: Shows Nationalists claiming traditional culture while Communists rejected it

keyPassages.quote: My fellow women: do not hate men! Hate that you do not have food to eat.
keyPassages.source: He Zhen, 'What Women Should Know About Communism'

keyPassages.quote: A constitutional republic which does not derive from the conscious realization and voluntary action of the majority of the people is a bogus republic.
We must preserve order, emphasize organization, responsibility and discipline, and be ready to die for the country at any moment.
Coming home from Europe, America or Japan, they can only parrot things foreign. They become gramophones.

people: He Zhen - anarchist-feminist, wrote in exile in Japan
Chen Duxiu - May Fourth leader, CCP co-founder (1921), expelled 1929
Chiang Kai-shek - Nationalist leader, New Life Movement
Mao Zedong - CCP leader, Yan'an Rectification
places: Shanghai - industrial center, He Zhen describes factory workers
Yan'an - CCP base (1935-1947), site of Rectification
Tokyo - exile location for Chinese anarchists including He Zhen
things: New Youth magazine - Chen Duxiu's journal, center of May Fourth debate
Rectification Campaign - ideological purification, thought reform
Rice bowl metaphor - economic dependence, survival

keyQuestions.question: He Zhen argues women should 'not hate men' but hate that they have no food. How does her analysis compare to contemporary feminism?
keyQuestions.talkingPoints: She rejects individual solutions (education, employment) as shifting dependence. Compare to liberal feminism ('lean in') vs. socialist feminism. Economic dependence as root of gendered oppression.

keyQuestions.question: Chen says a republic without changed consciousness is 'bogus.' What does this imply about the 1911 Revolution and later reforms?
keyQuestions.talkingPoints: 1911 Revolution changed institutions but not mentalities. New Culture Movement tried to change consciousness first. Chen would co-found CCP in 1921 - did he give up on this approach?

keyQuestions.question: How do Chiang's four virtues (li, yi, lian, chi) compare to Confucian originals? Is this genuine tradition or appropriation?
keyQuestions.talkingPoints: Chiang redefines terms: li becomes 'regulated attitude,' chi becomes 'self-consciousness.' Context: Nationalists competing with Communists who rejected tradition. New Life Movement failed; why?

keyQuestions.question: Mao attacks intellectuals who 'cannot open their mouths without citing ancient Greece.' What are the dangers of his anti-intellectualism?
keyQuestions.talkingPoints: Legitimate critique of theory divorced from practice. But also: establishes Mao as arbiter of correct thought. Pattern repeats in Anti-Rightist Campaign (1957), Cultural Revolution (1966). Knowledge as threat to power.

timeline.event: He Zhen and Liu Shipei publish Natural Justice in Japan
Chen Duxiu founds New Youth magazine
Chinese Communist Party founded (Chen Duxiu chair)
Chiang Kai-shek's Northern Expedition; Shanghai Massacre of Communists
Chiang launches New Life Movement; CCP begins Long March
CCP establishes Yan'an base; Mao consolidates leadership
Mao delivers 'Reform Our Study'; Rectification Campaign begins

connections.fromPrevious: Plate 20 covered the Pacific War and Japan's aggression. Now we see what was happening INSIDE China: civil war between Nationalists and Communists, competing visions for China's future.
connections.toNext: Plate 22 covers rebuilding Japan under American occupation. Compare: Japan rebuilt with US help; China rebuilt under Communist revolution. Different paths to post-war modernity.
connections.broaderThemes: What is modernity? Is it moral reform (Chiang), consciousness change (Chen), economic transformation (He Zhen), or adapting foreign theory to local conditions (Mao)? These debates continue today.

nextPreview.preview: American occupation (1945-1952) transformed Japan: new constitution, land reform, demilitarization. How did defeated Japan become an economic miracle?

teachingNotes: Four documents, four competing visions - use as comparison exercise
He Zhen's 'rice bowl' metaphor is vivid and memorable
Mao's 'gramophones' insult resonates with students who've encountered jargon-heavy academics
Chiang's New Life Movement failed spectacularly - ask why moral campaigns don't address structural problems
Chen co-founded CCP but was expelled - revolution devours its founders
Connect to contemporary China: Xi Jinping thought, ideological campaigns, 'core socialist values'
